seoTitle: XGHC Crystal Products | Crystal Resonators & Oscillators | LiTong Distributor
seoDescription: Explore XGHC crystal frequency products including quartz crystal resonators, crystal oscillators (TCXO/VCXO/OCXO), and automotive-grade crystals. Authorized distributor with technical support.

faqs.question: What are the main product categories offered by XGHC?
faqs.answer: XGHC offers four main product categories: (1) Crystal Resonators - passive quartz crystals in HC-49/S and SMD packages (3225, 2520, 2016, 1612) for clock generation; (2) Crystal Oscillators - active components including SPXO, TCXO, VCXO, and OCXO with built-in oscillator circuits; (3) Automotive Grade Crystals - AEC-Q200 qualified components for automotive applications; (4) Custom Crystal Solutions - specialized frequencies and specifications for unique applications. Each category includes multiple series optimized for specific frequency ranges, stability requirements, and operating environments.
faqs.decisionGuide: Review each product category to find the XGHC crystal component matching your frequency and accuracy requirements.

faqs.question: How do I select the right XGHC crystal for my application?
faqs.answer: Selecting the right XGHC crystal requires considering: (1) Frequency - match your system clock requirement exactly; (2) Frequency tolerance - ±10ppm to ±50ppm depending on accuracy needs; (3) Load capacitance (CL) - match your circuit design (typically 8pF to 20pF for resonators); (4) Package type - HC-49/S for through-hole, 3225/2520/2016/1612 for SMD; (5) Operating temperature range - commercial (0°C to +70°C), industrial (-40°C to +85°C), or automotive (-40°C to +125°C); (6) Drive level - ensure sufficient but not excessive power for resonators; (7) Aging - long-term frequency drift requirements; (8) ESR - lower is better for easier oscillation. For oscillators, consider stability (ppm), output type (CMOS, LVDS, etc.), and voltage. Contact LiTong FAE for personalized crystal selection assistance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use our crystal selection guide or contact FAE with your frequency, accuracy, and application requirements.

faqs.question: What is the difference between crystal resonators and crystal oscillators?
faqs.answer: Crystal resonators (quartz crystals) are passive components that require external oscillator circuitry to generate a clock signal. They offer lower cost and lower power consumption but need additional components (inverter, load capacitors, resistors). Crystal oscillators (XO, TCXO, VCXO, OCXO) are active components with built-in oscillator circuitry that output a direct clock signal. They provide better frequency stability, easier integration, and faster time-to-market but at higher cost. XGHC crystal resonators are ideal for cost-sensitive designs with available oscillator circuits. XGHC crystal oscillators are recommended for better stability, faster development, or when oscillator circuit design is not practical. TCXO offers temperature compensation, VCXO provides voltage control, and OCXO delivers highest stability with oven control.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose XGHC crystal resonators for cost-sensitive applications. Choose XGHC crystal oscillators for better stability and faster integration.

faqs.question: Which XGHC products are suitable for automotive applications?
faqs.answer: For automotive applications, XGHC offers AEC-Q200 qualified crystal products: (1) Automotive crystal resonators - AEC-Q200 Grade 3 qualified, -40°C to +125°C operation, tight frequency tolerance (±10ppm to ±30ppm); (2) Automotive TCXO - temperature-compensated oscillators for GPS and communication systems with ±0.5ppm to ±2.5ppm stability; (3) Automotive VCXO - voltage-controlled oscillators for clock synchronization; (4) High-reliability packaging - enhanced mechanical robustness for automotive environments. Key automotive features include enhanced temperature stability, vibration resistance, EMC compliance, and long-term supply commitment. All automotive products undergo rigorous qualification testing including 1000+ temperature cycles and mechanical stress testing. IATF 16949 certified manufacturing ensures automotive quality standards.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our automotive FAE team for AEC-Q200 qualified crystal selection and qualification documentation.

faqs.question: What crystal package sizes does XGHC offer?
faqs.answer: XGHC offers crystal components in various package sizes: (1) HC-49/S - standard through-hole package, most cost-effective for low frequencies (3.2-100MHz); (2) HC-49/SMD - surface-mount version of HC-49 for mixed PCB designs; (3) 3225 (3.2mm x 2.5mm) - popular SMD size for general applications, widely used in industrial and consumer electronics; (4) 2520 (2.5mm x 2.0mm) - compact SMD for space-constrained designs; (5) 2016 (2.0mm x 1.6mm) - ultra-compact for portable devices; (6) 1612 (1.6mm x 1.2mm) - miniature for wearables and IoT applications. Smaller packages generally have higher ESR and tighter manufacturing tolerances. XGHC also offers specialized packages for specific applications. Choose package based on available PCB space, frequency requirements, and cost considerations.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select package size based on PCB space constraints and frequency requirements. 3225 is most popular for general use.

faqs.question: What frequency range does XGHC cover?
faqs.answer: XGHC crystal products cover a wide frequency range: (1) Crystal resonators - typically 3.2MHz to 100MHz in fundamental mode, up to 200MHz in 3rd overtone; (2) Standard crystal oscillators (SPXO) - 1MHz to 200MHz; (3) TCXO (Temperature Compensated) - 1MHz to 100MHz with ±0.5ppm to ±2.5ppm stability; (4) VCXO (Voltage Controlled) - 1MHz to 100MHz with pullability range; (5) OCXO (Oven Controlled) - 5MHz to 100MHz with ±10ppb to ±50ppb stability. Common standard frequencies include 32.768kHz (real-time clock), 4MHz, 8MHz, 12MHz, 16MHz, 24MHz, 25MHz, 26MHz, 27MHz, 32MHz, 48MHz, and 50MHz. XGHC maintains large inventory of standard frequencies. Custom frequencies available upon request with reasonable minimum order quantities and lead times.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select frequency based on your system requirements. Standard frequencies offer better availability and pricing.

faqs.question: What is load capacitance (CL) and why is it important?
faqs.answer: Load capacitance (CL) is the effective capacitance seen by the crystal in the oscillator circuit. It is critical for crystal resonators because: (1) Frequency accuracy - the crystal is calibrated to oscillate at specified frequency with specific CL; (2) Circuit matching - must match your MCU/IC's recommended CL for proper oscillation; (3) Crystal selection - choose crystal CL to match your circuit design; (4) Calculation - CL = (C1 x C2) / (C1 + C2) + Cstray, where C1 and C2 are external load capacitors and Cstray is stray capacitance (typically 3-5pF). Common CL values are 8pF, 10pF, 12pF, 16pF, 18pF, and 20pF. Using wrong CL causes frequency shift and potential oscillation issues. XGHC crystals are available in various CL values to match different circuit requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: Match crystal CL to your circuit design. Contact FAE for load capacitance calculation assistance.

faqs.question: What is frequency stability and how do I specify it?
faqs.answer: Frequency stability indicates how much the crystal frequency varies under different conditions: (1) Temperature stability - frequency variation over operating temperature range, specified in ppm (parts per million); (2) Initial tolerance - frequency accuracy at 25°C, typically ±10ppm to ±50ppm; (3) Aging - long-term frequency drift, usually ±1-5ppm per year; (4) Supply voltage stability - variation with voltage changes (for oscillators). XGHC crystal resonators typically offer ±10ppm to ±30ppm temperature stability. XGHC TCXO provides ±0.5ppm to ±2.5ppm stability. XGHC OCXO achieves ±10ppb to ±50ppb stability. Specify stability based on your system timing accuracy requirements. GPS and communication systems need <±1ppm. Consumer electronics typically tolerate ±20-50ppm.
faqs.decisionGuide: Specify stability based on system timing accuracy needs. Contact FAE for stability requirement analysis.

categories.name: Crystal Resonators
categories.description: XGHC crystal resonators are high-precision quartz crystals that provide stable frequency references for clock generation. Available in HC-49/S through-hole and compact SMD packages (3225, 2520, 2016, 1612) with frequencies from 3.2MHz to 100MHz and various load capacitance options.

selectionGuide.title: How to Select XGHC Crystal Resonators
selectionGuide.description: Comprehensive guide to selecting crystal resonators based on frequency, load capacitance, package, and stability requirements.

faqs.question: What is the difference between HC-49/S and SMD crystal packages?
faqs.answer: HC-49/S is the traditional through-hole crystal package, most cost-effective and suitable for low-frequency applications (3.2-30MHz). It offers excellent mechanical stability and easy hand soldering. SMD packages (3225, 2520, 2016, 1612) are surface-mount devices for automated assembly and space-constrained designs. 3225 is most popular for general use. Smaller packages (2016, 1612) are for wearables and portable devices but have higher ESR. HC-49/S is ideal for prototypes and low-cost applications. SMD is preferred for production and compact designs.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose HC-49/S for cost-sensitive through-hole designs. Choose SMD packages for automated assembly and space-constrained applications.

faqs.question: How do I calculate the correct load capacitors for my crystal?
faqs.answer: Load capacitor calculation: CL = (C1 × C2) / (C1 + C2) + Cstray. Where CL is crystal load capacitance specification, C1 and C2 are external load capacitors, and Cstray is stray capacitance (typically 3-5pF from PCB traces and IC pins). For symmetric circuit with C1 = C2: C1 = C2 = 2 × (CL - Cstray). Example: For CL = 12pF and Cstray = 4pF: C1 = C2 = 2 × (12 - 4) = 16pF. Use standard capacitor values close to calculated value. XGHC provides load capacitance calculation tools and application notes. Incorrect load capacitors cause frequency shift and may prevent oscillation.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use the load capacitance formula or contact FAE for calculation assistance. Verify with frequency measurement.

faqs.question: What is ESR and why does it matter for crystal selection?
faqs.answer: ESR (Equivalent Series Resistance) is the resistance of the crystal at resonance frequency. Lower ESR is better because: (1) Easier oscillation start-up - low ESR crystals start oscillating more reliably; (2) Lower power consumption - less energy lost in crystal; (3) Better frequency stability - less sensitivity to circuit variations; (4) Wider drive level margin - more tolerance to drive level variations. Typical ESR values: HC-49/S 10-100Ω, 3225 30-150Ω, 2520 40-200Ω, smaller packages have higher ESR. Your MCU/IC datasheet specifies maximum ESR. Select crystal with ESR well below maximum specification for reliable operation.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select crystal with ESR well below your IC's maximum specification. Contact FAE for ESR requirement analysis.

faqs.question: How do I ensure my crystal oscillator circuit will start reliably?
faqs.answer: Reliable crystal oscillation requires: (1) Correct load capacitors - match crystal CL specification; (2) Adequate drive level - sufficient but not excessive power (check crystal drive level spec); (3) Low ESR crystal - select crystal with ESR well below IC maximum; (4) Proper PCB layout - keep traces short, good grounding, minimize stray capacitance; (5) Clean power supply - minimize noise on oscillator power; (6) Correct gain margin - ensure inverter has adequate gain for oscillation; (7) Temperature considerations - verify operation across full temperature range. XGHC provides oscillator circuit design guides. Test with actual hardware across voltage and temperature extremes.
faqs.decisionGuide: Follow oscillator design best practices. Contact FAE for circuit review and troubleshooting.

faqs.question: What is crystal aging and how does it affect my design?
faqs.answer: Crystal aging is the gradual frequency change over time due to stress relaxation and mass transfer in the quartz. Typical aging rates: ±1 to ±5 ppm per year for standard crystals, better for high-quality units. Aging is logarithmic - most change occurs in first year. Factors affecting aging: temperature (higher temp accelerates aging), drive level (excessive drive increases aging), mechanical stress (mounting and thermal expansion). For designs requiring long-term accuracy: (1) specify tighter initial tolerance; (2) allow for calibration; (3) use TCXO for critical applications; (4) consider aging in system design. XGHC provides aging specifications for all crystal series.
faqs.decisionGuide: Consider aging for long-term accuracy requirements. Use TCXO for critical timing applications.

products.name: HC-49S Crystal Resonator 8MHz
products.shortDescription: Standard HC-49/S through-hole crystal resonator, 8MHz frequency, 20pF load capacitance, ±30ppm tolerance, ideal for m...
descriptionParagraphs: The HC-49S-8MHz-20pF is a high-quality quartz crystal resonator in the standard HC-49/S through-hole package. It provides a stable 8MHz frequency reference with ±30ppm tolerance, suitable for microcontroller clock and general timing applications.

This crystal features 20pF load capacitance, compatible with most 8-bit and 32-bit microcontrollers. The HC-49/S package offers excellent mechanical stability and is ideal for through-hole PCB designs, prototypes, and cost-sensitive applications.

With low ESR and reliable oscillation characteristics, this crystal ensures stable clock generation across the commercial temperature range of 0°C to +70°C. It is widely used in consumer electronics, industrial control, and communication devices.
features: Standard HC-49/S package for easy prototyping

faeReview.author: Michael Zhang
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Timing Components
faeReview.content: The HC-49S-8MHz-20pF is my go-to recommendation for 8-bit microcontroller applications. The HC-49/S package is reliable and cost-effective. I have used this crystal in hundreds of customer designs with excellent results. The 20pF load capacitance works well with most MCUs including STM8, STM32, and PIC. For reliable oscillation, I recommend 22pF load capacitors with typical PCB stray capacitance. The ±30ppm tolerance is sufficient for UART communication up to 115200 baud. For applications requiring tighter tolerance, XGHC offers ±10ppm and ±20ppm options. This crystal is ideal for cost-sensitive consumer products where through-hole mounting is acceptable.
faeReview.highlight: Reliable 8MHz crystal for microcontroller applications with excellent cost-performance ratio

alternativeParts.reason: SMD alternative for automated assembly and space-constrained designs
alternativeParts.useCase: Choose 3225 package for production boards with automated assembly

alternativeParts.reason: Higher precision version for applications requiring better frequency accuracy
alternativeParts.useCase: Choose ±10ppm version for communication or timing-critical applications

companionParts.description: Load capacitors for crystal oscillator circuit (use two)
Feedback resistor for Pierce oscillator configuration
Popular ARM Cortex-M3 MCU compatible with 8MHz crystal
Socket for easy crystal replacement during development
Evaluation board for crystal oscillator testing

faqs.question: What load capacitors should I use with this crystal?
faqs.answer: For HC-49S-8MHz-20pF with CL=20pF, use two 22pF ceramic capacitors (C1 and C2). The calculation is: C1 = C2 = 2 × (CL - Cstray), where Cstray is typically 3-5pF. So C1 = C2 = 2 × (20 - 4) = 32pF, use standard 33pF or 22pF. Actual value may need fine-tuning based on PCB layout and stray capacitance. Use C0G/NP0 ceramic capacitors for best temperature stability. Verify actual frequency with frequency counter and adjust capacitors if needed.
faqs.decisionGuide: Start with 22pF capacitors and verify frequency. Adjust if measured frequency is off.

faqs.question: Can I use this crystal for UART communication?
faqs.answer: Yes, HC-49S-8MHz-20pF with ±30ppm tolerance is suitable for UART communication. At 8MHz, ±30ppm equals ±240Hz frequency variation. For standard UART baud rates, this provides adequate accuracy. For 115200 baud, the error from crystal tolerance is well within acceptable limits (<2%). However, for maximum baud rate accuracy or multi-device communication, consider ±20ppm or ±10ppm tolerance versions. Also ensure your PCB layout follows best practices for reliable oscillation. Test communication across temperature range if operating in varying environments.
faqs.decisionGuide: ±30ppm is sufficient for most UART applications. Choose tighter tolerance for maximum accuracy.

faqs.question: What is the maximum drive level for this crystal?
faqs.answer: The HC-49S-8MHz-20pF has a maximum drive level of 100μW. Exceeding this can cause: (1) Frequency shift due to thermal effects; (2) Accelerated aging; (3) Potential crystal damage in extreme cases. Typical microcontroller oscillator circuits drive crystals at 10-50μW, well within safe limits. To check drive level: measure crystal current with current probe, calculate power = I² × ESR. If drive level is too high, increase load capacitors or add series resistor. If too low (difficult start-up), decrease load capacitors. XGHC crystals are designed for reliable operation at standard MCU drive levels.
faqs.decisionGuide: Standard MCU circuits typically provide safe drive levels. Contact FAE if you suspect drive level issues.

faqs.question: Can this crystal operate at industrial temperature range?
faqs.answer: The standard HC-49S-8MHz-20pF is rated for 0°C to +70°C commercial temperature range. For industrial applications (-40°C to +85°C), XGHC offers industrial grade versions with extended temperature range and tighter temperature stability. The industrial version maintains reliable oscillation across the full industrial temperature range with slightly different specifications. For automotive applications (-40°C to +125°C), AEC-Q200 qualified versions are available. Specify temperature range when ordering to ensure correct part number.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose industrial grade for -40°C to +85°C operation. Contact FAE for industrial temperature options.

faqs.question: How do I verify the crystal is oscillating correctly?
faqs.answer: To verify crystal oscillation: (1) Check MCU clock output pin with oscilloscope - should see clean 8MHz square wave; (2) Measure frequency with frequency counter - should be within tolerance (8MHz ±30ppm = 7.99976 to 8.00024 MHz); (3) Check MCU is executing code - blinking LED or UART output; (4) Verify oscillation amplitude - typically 0.5-1V peak-to-peak at crystal pins; (5) Check for stable waveform - no jitter or noise. If no oscillation: check load capacitors, verify PCB layout, check for solder shorts, ensure adequate drive level. XGHC crystals are 100% tested before shipment.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use oscilloscope and frequency counter to verify oscillation. Contact FAE for troubleshooting assistance.

products.name: 3225 SMD Crystal Resonator 25MHz
products.shortDescription: Compact 3225 SMD crystal resonator, 25MHz frequency, 12pF load capacitance, ±20ppm tolerance, ideal for high-speed mi...
descriptionParagraphs: The 3225-25MHz-12pF is a compact surface-mount crystal resonator in the popular 3.2mm x 2.5mm package. It provides a stable 25MHz frequency reference with ±20ppm tolerance, suitable for high-speed microcontrollers, Ethernet applications, and communication interfaces.

This crystal features 12pF load capacitance, optimized for modern high-speed MCUs and SoCs. The 3225 package offers excellent balance between size and performance, making it ideal for space-constrained designs while maintaining good ESR characteristics.

With industrial temperature range of -40°C to +85°C and reliable ceramic package construction, this crystal is widely used in networking equipment, industrial control, and consumer electronics requiring higher clock frequencies.
features: Compact 3225 SMD package for space-constrained designs

faeReview.author: David Liu
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Communication Components
faeReview.content: The 3225-25MHz-12pF is my recommendation for Ethernet and high-speed MCU applications. The 3225 package is the sweet spot - small enough for compact designs but large enough for good ESR and easy handling. I have used this crystal extensively in Ethernet PHY clock applications with excellent results. The 12pF load capacitance works well with most modern ARM Cortex-M4/M7 MCUs and Ethernet PHYs. For 25MHz Ethernet RMII clock, the ±20ppm tolerance provides adequate accuracy. PCB layout is critical at 25MHz - keep traces short and symmetric. I recommend 15pF load capacitors for typical 3-4pF stray capacitance. This crystal is also popular for WiFi modules requiring 25MHz or 26MHz reference.
faeReview.highlight: Excellent 25MHz crystal for Ethernet and high-speed MCU applications in compact SMD package

alternativeParts.reason: Higher precision version for applications requiring better frequency accuracy
alternativeParts.useCase: Choose ±10ppm version for precision Ethernet or synchronous communication
Choose 2520 package when board space is extremely limited

companionParts.description: Load capacitors for 12pF CL crystal (use two)
10/100 Ethernet PHY using 25MHz crystal
High-performance ARM Cortex-M4 MCU
WiFi/Bluetooth SoC using 40MHz crystal (25MHz variant available)
Test socket for 3225 package crystals

faqs.question: What load capacitors should I use with this 12pF CL crystal?
faqs.answer: For 3225-25MHz-12pF with CL=12pF, use two 15pF ceramic capacitors. The calculation: C1 = C2 = 2 × (CL - Cstray). With typical Cstray = 3-4pF: C1 = C2 = 2 × (12 - 3.5) = 17pF. Use standard 15pF or 18pF values. At 25MHz, proper load capacitance is critical for stable oscillation. Use C0G/NP0 ceramic capacitors with tight tolerance (±5% or better). Place capacitors close to crystal pins with short traces. Verify actual frequency with frequency counter and adjust if measured frequency deviates significantly from 25MHz.
faqs.decisionGuide: Start with 15pF capacitors for 12pF CL crystal. Verify frequency and adjust if needed.

faqs.question: Is this crystal suitable for Ethernet applications?
faqs.answer: Yes, 3225-25MHz-12pF is excellent for 10/100 Ethernet PHY clock applications. Most Ethernet PHYs like DP83848, KSZ8081, and LAN8720 use 25MHz crystal reference. The ±20ppm tolerance provides adequate accuracy for Ethernet communication. The 3225 package is widely used in Ethernet designs. Ensure your PHY datasheet specifies 25MHz crystal compatibility. Some PHYs require specific load capacitance - verify CL matches your PHY requirements (12pF is common). For Gigabit Ethernet, check if 25MHz is the correct frequency - some Gigabit PHYs use different frequencies. Layout is critical - place crystal close to PHY with short traces.
faqs.decisionGuide: Verify your Ethernet PHY datasheet for 25MHz crystal compatibility. This crystal is suitable for most 10/100 Ethernet applications.

faqs.question: What is the advantage of 3225 package over smaller sizes?
faqs.answer: The 3225 (3.2mm x 2.5mm) package offers several advantages over smaller packages: (1) Lower ESR - typically 60-80Ω vs 100-150Ω for 2520/2016; (2) Easier handling - larger size is easier to place and inspect; (3) Better availability - 3225 is the most popular size with best stock; (4) Cost-effective - good balance of size and price; (5) Reliable soldering - larger pads are more forgiving; (6) Good frequency range - supports 8MHz to 50MHz well. Smaller packages (2520, 2016, 1612) are better for space-constrained wearables but have higher ESR and may be harder to source. 3225 is the sweet spot for most applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose 3225 for best balance of performance and size. Use smaller packages only when space is critical.

faqs.question: Can I use this crystal with 3.3V or 5V MCUs?
faqs.answer: Yes, 3225-25MHz-12pF works with both 3.3V and 5V MCUs. The crystal itself is a passive component and doesn't have voltage rating. The MCU's internal oscillator inverter operates at the MCU's supply voltage. Most modern MCUs have oscillator circuits that work across 1.8V to 5.5V. Key considerations: (1) Ensure your MCU supports 25MHz at its operating voltage; (2) Check MCU datasheet for recommended crystal specifications; (3) Verify load capacitance matches MCU recommendations; (4) Some 5V MCUs may have different drive levels - verify crystal drive level is within specification. XGHC crystals work reliably with both 3.3V and 5V systems when properly designed.
faqs.decisionGuide: This crystal works with both 3.3V and 5V systems. Verify your MCU supports 25MHz operation.

faqs.question: What PCB layout recommendations apply to this crystal?
faqs.answer: PCB layout recommendations for 3225 crystal: (1) Place crystal as close as possible to MCU crystal pins - minimize trace length; (2) Keep traces symmetric - equal length and width between crystal and MCU; (3) Use ground plane under crystal - provides isolation and reduces noise; (4) Keep high-speed signals away - avoid routing switching signals near crystal traces; (5) Place load capacitors close to crystal - minimize trace length; (6) Use guard ring if needed - for noisy environments; (7) Avoid vias on crystal traces - keep traces on single layer if possible; (8) Keep loop area small - minimize EMI pickup. At 25MHz, layout is more critical than at lower frequencies. Follow these guidelines for reliable oscillation.
faqs.decisionGuide: Follow PCB layout best practices. Keep traces short and symmetric. Contact FAE for layout review.

categories.name: Crystal Oscillators
categories.description: XGHC crystal oscillators are active frequency components with built-in oscillator circuitry providing direct clock output. Available types include SPXO (Standard), TCXO (Temperature Compensated), VCXO (Voltage Controlled), and OCXO (Oven Controlled) for various stability and control requirements.

selectionGuide.title: How to Select XGHC Crystal Oscillators
selectionGuide.description: Guide to selecting crystal oscillators based on stability requirements, output type, and application needs.

faqs.question: What types of crystal oscillators does XGHC offer?
faqs.answer: XGHC offers four main types of crystal oscillators: (1) SPXO (Standard Crystal Oscillator) - basic oscillator with ±25-100ppm stability, lowest cost; (2) TCXO (Temperature Compensated) - includes temperature compensation circuit for ±0.5-2.5ppm stability; (3) VCXO (Voltage Controlled) - frequency can be adjusted by control voltage for synchronization; (4) OCXO (Oven Controlled) - heated crystal chamber for ±10-50ppb stability, highest precision. SPXO is suitable for general applications. TCXO is ideal for communication and GPS. VCXO is used for clock synchronization. OCXO is for precision timing and instrumentation.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose SPXO for cost-sensitive applications. Choose TCXO for better temperature stability. Choose VCXO for frequency control. Choose OCXO for highest precision.

faqs.question: When should I use a crystal oscillator instead of a crystal resonator?
faqs.answer: Use XGHC crystal oscillators instead of resonators when: (1) Faster time-to-market - no oscillator circuit design needed; (2) Better frequency stability - active compensation in TCXO/OCXO; (3) Guaranteed oscillation - factory-tested reliable start-up; (4) Space savings - no external oscillator components; (5) Higher frequencies - easier to achieve >50MHz; (6) Multiple outputs - some oscillators provide multiple clock phases; (7) Specific output format - LVDS, LVPECL, HCMOS options. Crystal resonators are preferred for: cost-sensitive designs, low-power applications, custom oscillator requirements, or when board space for oscillator circuit is available. XGHC offers both options to match your needs.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose oscillators for faster development and better stability. Choose resonators for cost savings and lower power.

faqs.question: What output types are available for XGHC crystal oscillators?
faqs.answer: XGHC crystal oscillators offer various output types: (1) CMOS/HCMOS - most common, rail-to-rail square wave, compatible with digital logic; (2) LVCMOS - low-voltage CMOS for 1.8V/2.5V systems; (3) LVDS (Low Voltage Differential Signaling) - differential output for high-speed, low-noise applications; (4) LVPECL (Low Voltage Positive ECL) - differential output for high-frequency applications; (5) HCSL (High-Speed Current Steering Logic) - used for PCIe and high-speed serial; (6) Sine wave - analog output for RF applications. CMOS is most common and widely compatible. LVDS/LVPECL are for high-speed differential signaling. Choose based on your load requirements and signal integrity needs.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose CMOS for general applications. Choose LVDS/LVPECL for high-speed differential signaling.

faqs.question: What is the difference between TCXO and OCXO?
faqs.answer: TCXO (Temperature Compensated Crystal Oscillator) and OCXO (Oven Controlled Crystal Oscillator) provide improved stability: TCXO uses electronic temperature compensation to achieve ±0.5-2.5ppm stability across temperature. It has faster warm-up, lower power consumption, and smaller size. OCXO uses a heated enclosure to maintain constant crystal temperature, achieving ±10-50ppb stability. It has longer warm-up (minutes), higher power consumption, and larger size. Choose TCXO for communication, GPS, and most precision applications. Choose OCXO for instrumentation, reference clocks, and highest precision timing. XGHC offers both types with various stability grades.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose TCXO for most precision applications. Choose OCXO for highest precision and reference applications.

faqs.question: How do I power and interface with crystal oscillators?
faqs.answer: XGHC crystal oscillators are easy to interface: Power supply - connect VDD to specified voltage (1.8V, 2.5V, 3.3V, or 5V), bypass with 0.1μF and 10μF capacitors close to device. Ground - connect all GND pins to solid ground plane. Output - connect to clock input of your IC. Enable pin (if present) - tie high for normal operation or control for power saving. No external components needed for oscillation. Layout tips: keep power traces short, use adequate decoupling, avoid routing output near sensitive analog circuits. Oscillators are factory-tested and guaranteed to start reliably. XGHC oscillators include output enable/disable and sometimes standby modes.
faqs.decisionGuide: Connect power, ground, and output. Add decoupling capacitors. No external oscillator circuit needed.

products.name: 25MHz CMOS Crystal Oscillator
products.shortDescription: Standard crystal oscillator, 25MHz frequency, 3.3V operation, CMOS output, ±50ppm stability, 5.0x3.2mm ceramic packag...
descriptionParagraphs: The XO-25MHz-3.3V-CMOS is a standard crystal oscillator providing a stable 25MHz clock output with CMOS logic levels. It operates from 3.3V supply and provides rail-to-rail square wave output compatible with standard digital logic.

This oscillator features ±50ppm total stability including temperature variation and initial tolerance. The built-in oscillator circuit ensures reliable start-up and consistent performance without external components.
Housed in a compact 5.0mm x 3.2mm ceramic SMD package, this oscillator is ideal for applications requiring a simple clock solution without the need for crystal oscillator circuit design.

faeReview.author: Steven Wang
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Timing Solutions
faeReview.content: The XO-25MHz-3.3V-CMOS is my recommendation for customers who need a quick, reliable clock solution without designing an oscillator circuit. It's plug-and-play - just connect power and you have a clean 25MHz clock. I use this extensively for FPGA development boards and Ethernet applications. The ±50ppm stability is adequate for most digital applications. For customers transitioning from crystal resonators to oscillators, this is a great entry point. The 3.3V operation matches most modern MCUs and FPGAs. Power consumption is reasonable at 15mA. The enable pin is useful for power management - you can shut down the clock when not needed. Overall, an excellent general-purpose oscillator at a competitive price.
faeReview.highlight: Plug-and-play 25MHz oscillator with reliable performance and easy integration

alternativeParts.reason: Better stability version for applications requiring tighter frequency tolerance
alternativeParts.useCase: Choose ±25ppm version for communication or synchronous applications

alternativeParts.reason: Temperature compensated for much better stability across temperature
alternativeParts.useCase: Choose TCXO for GPS, communication, or precision applications

Test socket for 5.0x3.2mm oscillators

faqs.question: How do I connect this oscillator to my MCU?
faqs.answer: Connecting XO-25MHz-3.3V-CMOS is straightforward: (1) Connect VDD pin to 3.3V supply; (2) Connect GND pin to ground; (3) Connect OUT pin to MCU clock input; (4) Add 0.1μF decoupling capacitor between VDD and GND close to oscillator; (5) Connect OE (Output Enable) to VDD or leave floating for normal operation. No other components needed. The oscillator outputs CMOS levels (0V to 3.3V) compatible with 3.3V MCUs. For 5V MCUs, verify input high voltage is compatible (typically 0.7xVDD = 3.5V for 5V MCU). Some MCUs may need level shifting. Check your MCU datasheet for clock input requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: Connect VDD, GND, and OUT. Add decoupling capacitor. No external oscillator circuit needed.

faqs.question: What is the difference between this oscillator and a crystal resonator?
faqs.answer: Key differences between XO-25MHz oscillator and crystal resonator: (1) Active vs passive - oscillator has built-in circuit, resonator needs external oscillator; (2) Ease of use - oscillator is plug-and-play, resonator needs circuit design; (3) Power consumption - oscillator uses 15mA, resonator uses <1mA; (4) Cost - oscillator is more expensive; (5) Size - oscillator is larger (5x3.2mm vs 3.2x2.5mm crystal); (6) Stability - oscillator has guaranteed ±50ppm, resonator depends on circuit; (7) Start-up - oscillator starts in <10ms, resonator may need more time. Choose oscillator for faster development and guaranteed performance. Choose resonator for cost savings and lower power.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose oscillator for easy integration. Choose resonator for cost and power savings.

faqs.question: Can I use the output enable pin for power saving?
faqs.answer: Yes, the OE (Output Enable) pin can be used for power management: When OE is high or floating, oscillator operates normally with clock output. When OE is low, output is disabled (high impedance or low depending on model) and current consumption is reduced. This allows you to: (1) Gate the clock when not needed; (2) Reduce power in standby modes; (3) Synchronize multiple oscillators; (4) Switch between clock sources. Power savings in disable mode varies by model - typically 30-50% reduction. Check datasheet for specific OE pin behavior. Some oscillators have ST (Standby) pin instead of OE with different functionality.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use OE pin for clock gating and power saving. Connect to GPIO for software control.

faqs.question: What is the warm-up time for this oscillator?
faqs.answer: The XO-25MHz-3.3V-CMOS has typical warm-up time of <10ms from power-on to stable output. This is much faster than OCXO which can take minutes. The warm-up time includes: (1) Power supply stabilization - microseconds; (2) Internal oscillator start-up - milliseconds; (3) Output amplitude stabilization - milliseconds. For most applications, the oscillator is ready immediately after power-on. If precise timing is needed immediately after power-up, allow 10-50ms for full stabilization. Warm-up time is specified in the datasheet and is consistent across production. Temperature has minimal effect on warm-up time for standard oscillators.
faqs.decisionGuide: Oscillator is ready in <10ms. Allow 50ms for full stabilization if precise timing needed immediately.

faqs.question: Is this oscillator suitable for FPGA clock input?
faqs.answer: Yes, XO-25MHz-3.3V-CMOS is excellent for FPGA clock input. FPGAs like Xilinx, Intel/Altera, and Lattice typically accept CMOS-level clock inputs. Key considerations: (1) Voltage level - 3.3V CMOS is compatible with 3.3V FPGAs; (2) Frequency - 25MHz is within FPGA PLL input range; (3) Jitter - standard oscillators have adequate jitter for most FPGA applications; (4) Duty cycle - 45-55% is acceptable for FPGAs. For high-speed FPGA designs (>100MHz internal), consider lower jitter oscillators or TCXO. For clocking high-speed transceivers, use dedicated reference oscillators. Check FPGA datasheet for clock input specifications. XGHC oscillators are widely used in FPGA designs.
faqs.decisionGuide: Suitable for most FPGA applications. Check FPGA datasheet for specific clock requirements.

products.name: 26MHz TCXO 2.5ppm
products.shortDescription: Temperature compensated crystal oscillator, 26MHz frequency, ±2.5ppm stability, 3.3V operation, ideal for GPS and pre...
descriptionParagraphs: The TCXO-26MHz-3.3V-2.5ppm is a high-stability temperature compensated crystal oscillator providing exceptional frequency stability across temperature variations. It delivers ±2.5ppm stability from -30°C to +85°C.

This TCXO uses internal temperature compensation circuitry to counteract the natural frequency drift of the quartz crystal with temperature. The result is a stable clock source suitable for GPS receivers, cellular communication, and precision timing applications.

Operating from 3.3V supply with CMOS output, this TCXO is compatible with modern GPS and communication chipsets. The 26MHz frequency is standard for GPS reference and widely used in mobile communication.
features: CMOS output compatible with GPS/communication ICs

faeReview.author: James Chen
faeReview.title: Principal FAE - RF & Communication
faeReview.content: The TCXO-26MHz-3.3V-2.5ppm is my top recommendation for GPS and cellular applications. The ±2.5ppm stability is excellent for GPS receivers - it meets the requirements for most consumer and industrial GPS modules. I've used this TCXO in numerous GPS designs with u-blox, MTK, and Qualcomm chipsets with excellent results. The 26MHz frequency is the standard reference for GPS L1 band. For cellular, this stability supports LTE and 5G NR requirements. The 3.2x2.5mm package is compact enough for mobile devices. Power consumption of 25mA is reasonable for the performance. Warm-up time of 2 seconds is acceptable for most applications. If you need even better stability, XGHC offers ±1.0ppm and ±0.5ppm versions. Overall, an excellent TCXO for precision timing applications.
faeReview.highlight: High-stability TCXO ideal for GPS and cellular communication applications

alternativeParts.reason: Higher precision TCXO for demanding GPS and communication applications
alternativeParts.useCase: Choose ±1.0ppm for high-precision GPS or carrier-grade communication

alternativeParts.reason: Lower cost alternative when ±2.5ppm stability is not required
alternativeParts.useCase: Choose SPXO for cost-sensitive applications with less stringent stability needs

companionParts.description: Decoupling capacitor for TCXO power supply
u-blox GPS module using 26MHz TCXO reference
4G LTE module with 26MHz reference input
Evaluation board for TCXO testing
Precision frequency counter for stability measurement

faqs.question: What makes TCXO better than standard crystal oscillators?
faqs.answer: TCXO (Temperature Compensated Crystal Oscillator) provides much better frequency stability than standard oscillators: Standard XO has ±25-100ppm stability varying with temperature. TCXO has ±0.5-2.5ppm stability across temperature. This 10-50x improvement comes from internal temperature compensation circuitry that adjusts oscillator frequency based on temperature sensor readings. TCXO is essential for: GPS receivers (need <±2.5ppm for reliable lock), cellular communication (tight frequency requirements), precision timing, and any application where frequency drift affects performance. The trade-offs are higher cost, higher power consumption, and slightly larger size. For applications requiring stability across temperature, TCXO is the solution.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose TCXO when frequency stability across temperature is critical. Standard XO is sufficient for less demanding applications.

faqs.question: Is this TCXO suitable for GPS applications?
faqs.answer: Yes, TCXO-26MHz-3.3V-2.5ppm is excellent for GPS applications. GPS receivers require stable reference frequency for accurate position calculation. Key requirements: (1) Frequency - 26MHz is standard reference for GPS L1 band (1575.42MHz); (2) Stability - ±2.5ppm provides adequate stability for consumer and industrial GPS; (3) Temperature range - -30°C to +85°C covers most operating environments; (4) Warm-up - 2 seconds is acceptable for GPS cold start. For high-precision GPS (RTK, surveying), consider ±1.0ppm or ±0.5ppm versions. For basic navigation and tracking, ±2.5ppm is sufficient. This TCXO is compatible with popular GPS modules from u-blox, MTK, Qualcomm, and others. Many successful GPS designs use this TCXO.
faqs.decisionGuide: ±2.5ppm TCXO is suitable for most GPS applications. Choose tighter tolerance for high-precision GPS.

faqs.question: What is the warm-up time and why does it matter?
faqs.answer: TCXO warm-up time is the time from power-on to stable frequency output. For TCXO-26MHz-3.3V-2.5ppm, warm-up time is <2 seconds. This includes: (1) Oscillator start-up - milliseconds; (2) Temperature sensor stabilization - hundreds of milliseconds; (3) Compensation circuit settling - seconds. Warm-up time matters for: (1) GPS cold start - affects time-to-first-fix; (2) Communication systems - may affect initial synchronization; (3) Precision measurement - wait for stable frequency before calibration. After warm-up, frequency is stable within specification. Some applications can operate during warm-up with reduced accuracy. For faster warm-up, consider specific fast-warm-up TCXO models. OCXO has much longer warm-up (minutes) due to oven heating.
faqs.decisionGuide: Allow 2 seconds for TCXO warm-up. Consider fast-warm-up models if quicker stabilization needed.

faqs.question: How do I verify the TCXO stability meets specification?
faqs.answer: To verify TCXO stability: (1) Frequency accuracy - measure frequency at 25°C with precision counter, should be within ±2.5ppm of 26MHz; (2) Temperature stability - measure frequency across temperature range (-30°C to +85°C), variation should be <±2.5ppm; (3) Allan deviation - for short-term stability measurement; (4) Phase noise - for jitter characterization. Test equipment needed: precision frequency counter (0.1ppm resolution), temperature chamber, spectrum analyzer (for phase noise). XGHC TCXOs are 100% tested before shipment. For production, sample testing is usually sufficient. For critical applications, full characterization may be warranted. Contact XGHC for detailed test reports.
faqs.decisionGuide: Verify with precision frequency counter. Contact XGHC for detailed test reports and characterization data.

faqs.question: Can I use this TCXO for cellular LTE/5G applications?
faqs.answer: Yes, TCXO-26MHz-3.3V-2.5ppm is suitable for cellular LTE and 5G applications. Cellular systems require precise frequency reference for: (1) Carrier frequency accuracy - affects transmit/receive frequency; (2) Symbol timing - affects data decoding; (3) Handoff performance - affects switching between cells. LTE requirements: typically ±0.1ppm for carrier frequency. This TCXO's ±2.5ppm is sufficient for most LTE user equipment when combined with network synchronization. 5G NR has similar or slightly tighter requirements. The 26MHz frequency is commonly used in cellular baseband processors. For cellular infrastructure (base stations), tighter stability may be required. For mobile devices and IoT, this TCXO meets requirements. Check your cellular chipset datasheet for specific reference requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: Suitable for LTE/5G user equipment. Check cellular chipset datasheet for specific requirements.

categories.name: Automotive Grade Crystals
categories.description: XGHC automotive grade crystal components are AEC-Q200 qualified for automotive applications. These crystals meet stringent automotive reliability standards with enhanced temperature range, vibration resistance, and long-term stability for infotainment, ADAS, and body electronics.

selectionGuide.title: How to Select Automotive Grade Crystals
selectionGuide.description: Guide to selecting AEC-Q200 qualified crystals for automotive applications based on reliability and environmental requirements.

faqs.question: What is AEC-Q200 qualification?
faqs.answer: AEC-Q200 is the Automotive Electronics Council qualification standard for passive electronic components. It defines rigorous testing requirements including: (1) Temperature cycling - typically 1000 cycles from -40°C to +125°C or +150°C; (2) High temperature storage - 1000 hours at maximum temperature; (3) Mechanical shock and vibration - simulating automotive environment; (4) Humidity testing - high humidity with bias; (5) Solderability - ensuring reliable PCB attachment. AEC-Q200 has different grades: Grade 0 (-40 to +150°C), Grade 1 (-40 to +125°C), Grade 2 (-40 to +105°C), Grade 3 (-40 to +85°C). XGHC automotive crystals are AEC-Q200 qualified, ensuring they meet automotive reliability requirements. IATF 16949 certified manufacturing ensures consistent quality.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose AEC-Q200 qualified crystals for all automotive applications. Verify grade matches your temperature requirements.

faqs.question: What temperature range do automotive crystals support?
faqs.answer: XGHC automotive crystals support extended temperature ranges: Grade 3: -40°C to +85°C for passenger compartment electronics; Grade 2: -40°C to +105°C for general automotive under-hood; Grade 1: -40°C to +125°C for powertrain and harsh environments; Grade 0: -40°C to +150°C for extreme applications. Standard consumer crystals (0°C to +70°C) are not suitable for automotive. Automotive crystals maintain frequency stability across the full temperature range. Tighter frequency tolerance is often specified for automotive to ensure reliable communication and timing. Select grade based on installation location and operating environment. Most automotive applications use Grade 1 or Grade 2.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select AEC-Q200 grade based on operating environment. Grade 1 (-40 to +125°C) is most common for automotive.

faqs.question: Why are automotive crystals more expensive than standard crystals?
faqs.answer: Automotive crystals cost more due to: (1) Enhanced qualification - AEC-Q200 testing is extensive and expensive; (2) Higher grade materials - materials rated for wider temperature range; (3) Stricter manufacturing controls - IATF 16949 automotive quality system; (4) Traceability - full lot traceability for automotive supply chain; (5) Lower failure rate requirements - automotive requires higher reliability; (6) Long-term supply commitment - typically 10-15 year production guarantee; (7) Documentation - PPAP (Production Part Approval Process) documentation. The cost premium is typically 20-50% over commercial grade. This investment ensures reliable operation in automotive environments and compliance with OEM requirements. For automotive applications, using non-automotive grade crystals is not recommended due to reliability risks.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use AEC-Q200 qualified crystals for automotive. The cost premium ensures reliability and compliance.

faqs.question: What documentation comes with automotive crystals?
faqs.answer: XGHC automotive crystals include comprehensive documentation: (1) AEC-Q200 test report - detailed qualification test results; (2) PPAP documentation - Production Part Approval Process package including design records, material certifications, process flow, control plan, and measurement results; (3) IMDS entry - International Material Data System for environmental compliance; (4) Certificate of conformance - confirming parts meet specifications; (5) Lot traceability information - for supply chain tracking; (6) Reliability data - FIT rates and lifetime predictions; (7) Application notes - automotive-specific design guidelines. This documentation is required by automotive OEMs and Tier 1 suppliers. Contact LiTong for complete automotive documentation package.
faqs.decisionGuide: Request complete PPAP package and AEC-Q200 reports for automotive qualification. Contact LiTong automotive FAE.

faqs.question: Are automotive crystals pin-compatible with standard crystals?
faqs.answer: Yes, XGHC automotive crystals are pin-compatible with standard crystals of the same package. A 3225 automotive crystal has the same pinout as a 3225 commercial crystal. The differences are internal: higher grade quartz, enhanced sealing, automotive-grade materials, and tighter manufacturing controls. This means you can use automotive crystals in standard applications for higher reliability, but you cannot use standard crystals in automotive applications. For development, you can use commercial crystals, but for production automotive units, you must use AEC-Q200 qualified parts. Always verify the specific part number includes automotive qualification. XGHC automotive part numbers typically have 'A' suffix or specific automotive series designation.
faqs.decisionGuide: Automotive crystals are drop-in replacements for standard crystals. Use automotive grade for automotive applications.

products.name: AEC-Q200 Grade 1 Crystal 16MHz
products.shortDescription: Automotive grade crystal resonator, AEC-Q200 Grade 1 qualified, 16MHz frequency, -40°C to +125°C, 3225 SMD package fo...
descriptionParagraphs: The 3225-16MHz-AECQ200-G1 is an AEC-Q200 Grade 1 qualified crystal resonator designed for automotive applications. It provides reliable 16MHz frequency reference across the automotive temperature range of -40°C to +125°C.

This crystal has passed rigorous AEC-Q200 qualification testing including 1000 temperature cycles, mechanical shock, vibration, and humidity testing. It meets the stringent reliability requirements of automotive OEMs and Tier 1 suppliers.

The 3225 SMD package is compatible with automated assembly processes used in automotive manufacturing. IATF 16949 certified production ensures consistent quality and full traceability for automotive supply chain requirements.

faeReview.author: Robert Chen
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Automotive Electronics
faeReview.content: The 3225-16MHz-AECQ200-G1 is my standard recommendation for automotive microcontroller clock applications. The AEC-Q200 Grade 1 qualification is essential for automotive - it ensures the crystal will survive the harsh automotive environment. I have used this crystal in numerous automotive projects including infotainment, body control, and ADAS applications. The 16MHz frequency is standard for many automotive MCUs including NXP S32K, Infineon Aurix, and Renesas RH850. The -40°C to +125°C range covers most automotive applications except extreme under-hood locations. The PPAP documentation package is comprehensive and accepted by major OEMs. For powertrain applications requiring Grade 0 (-40 to +150°C), XGHC offers that option too. Always use AEC-Q200 qualified crystals in automotive - using commercial grade is a reliability risk.
faeReview.highlight: AEC-Q200 Grade 1 qualified crystal ideal for automotive MCU clock applications

alternativeParts.useCase: Choose Grade 0 for under-hood powertrain applications with extreme temperatures

alternativeParts.useCase: Choose industrial grade for non-automotive wide-temp applications

Complete AEC-Q200 qualification documentation

faqs.question: What AEC-Q200 tests has this crystal passed?
faqs.answer: The 3225-16MHz-AECQ200-G1 has passed full AEC-Q200 Grade 1 qualification including: (1) High temperature storage - 1000 hours at 125°C; (2) Temperature cycling - 1000 cycles -40°C to +125°C; (3) Mechanical shock - 1500g half-sine pulse; (4) Vibration - variable frequency 5-2000Hz; (5) Moisture resistance - 85°C/85%RH with bias; (6) Solderability - wetting balance test; (7) Electrical characterization - across full temperature range; (8) Board flex - for SMD components; (9) Terminal strength - pull and shear tests. All tests passed per AEC-Q200 Rev E requirements. Full test reports available upon request. This qualification ensures the crystal will perform reliably in automotive applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Full AEC-Q200 qualification ensures automotive reliability. Request test reports for your documentation.

faqs.question: Can I use this crystal in powertrain applications?
faqs.answer: The 3225-16MHz-AECQ200-G1 is Grade 1 (-40°C to +125°C) qualified, suitable for most automotive applications including body electronics, infotainment, and instrument clusters. For powertrain applications (engine control, transmission), the environment may exceed +125°C. Check your specific powertrain location: Under-hood near engine - may reach 150°C, requires Grade 0; Transmission control - typically 125-150°C, may need Grade 0; Cabin-mounted ECUs - usually within Grade 1 range. If your powertrain application stays within -40°C to +125°C, Grade 1 is sufficient. For higher temperatures, use XGHC Grade 0 (-40°C to +150°C) version. Always verify the temperature profile of your specific installation location.
faqs.decisionGuide: Grade 1 is suitable for most automotive. Use Grade 0 for extreme under-hood powertrain locations.

faqs.question: What documentation is provided for automotive qualification?
faqs.answer: XGHC provides comprehensive automotive documentation: (1) AEC-Q200 Test Report - detailed results of all qualification tests; (2) PPAP Level 3 Package - includes design records, material certifications, process flow, FMEA, control plan, MSA, and dimensional results; (3) IMDS Entry - material composition for environmental compliance; (4) Certificate of Conformance - confirming parts meet specifications; (5) Lot Traceability - full supply chain tracking; (6) Reliability Report - FIT rates and lifetime predictions; (7) Application Note - automotive design guidelines; (8) Change Notification Agreement - PCN process documentation. This documentation package meets requirements of major automotive OEMs including Ford, GM, VW, Toyota, and others. Contact LiTong automotive FAE for complete documentation.
faqs.decisionGuide: Request complete PPAP package for automotive qualification. Contact automotive FAE for documentation.

faqs.question: How do I verify this is genuine AEC-Q200 qualified crystal?
faqs.answer: To verify genuine AEC-Q200 qualification: (1) Check part number - should include AEC-Q200 or automotive designation; (2) Request AEC-Q200 test report - genuine parts have complete test documentation; (3) Verify manufacturer - XGHC is recognized AEC-Q200 supplier; (4) Check distributor - LiTong is authorized XGHC distributor; (5) Review PPAP - authentic automotive parts have PPAP documentation; (6) Lot traceability - genuine parts have full traceability; (7) Quality certificates - IATF 16949 manufacturing. Counterfeit automotive parts are a serious issue. Always purchase from authorized distributors like LiTong. Request and review documentation before production. XGHC provides certificate of conformance with each shipment. Contact XGHC or LiTong if you have any doubts about authenticity.
faqs.decisionGuide: Purchase from authorized distributors. Request complete documentation. Verify part markings.

faqs.question: What is the long-term supply commitment for automotive crystals?
faqs.answer: XGHC provides long-term supply commitment for automotive crystals: (1) Production lifetime - typically 10-15 years from product launch; (2) Last-time-buy notification - minimum 12 months notice before discontinuation; (3) Form-fit-function replacements - if discontinuation necessary, compatible replacement provided; (4) PCN process - Product Change Notification per automotive requirements; (5) End-of-life management - support through typical automotive production cycles. This commitment aligns with automotive industry requirements where vehicles are supported for 10+ years. XGHC understands the critical importance of supply continuity in automotive. Contact LiTong for specific supply agreements and LTBP (Long Term Business Plan) documentation. For critical applications, consider multi-sourcing strategies.
faqs.decisionGuide: XGHC provides 10-15 year supply commitment for automotive. Contact FAE for supply agreements.
